judgments.comparative_verdict: Report 2 hits all four expectations while Report 1 misses the early-stage placement of F-11 and mischaracterizes the RESULTS_DB collision risk as "unused today," making Report 2 the stronger and more accurate answer overall.

report_1.evidence: Engineer B — `pipeline/registry.py`:** F-02, F-05, F-09. Three constants in one file, each a one-line change... give it to one owner.

report_2.evidence: Batch A — Config (`registry.py`): F-02, F-05, F-09.** All three edit the same file (`MODEL_ENDPOINTS`, `DEFAULT_RUBRIC_VERSION`, `DEFAULT_DATASET`); one owner does them in series.

report_1.evidence: Wave 2 — the two tests, after their fixes: A writes F-12 only after the pagination fix; C writes F-11 only after F-01.

report_2.evidence: Wave 1 — four fully-parallel batches, one owner each (files are disjoint, so no collisions): ... Batch C — Rubric (`rubric.py`): F-01, paired with F-11.

report_1.evidence: F-06 — a scoring-policy change, not a mechanical fix... Hand out the *decision* first, then the change.

report_2.evidence: F-06 — do not hand out as a blind fix; escalate for a decision... Per project documentation, flag it as a behavior change, escalate as its own item, and don't let the campaign guess.

report_1.evidence: tests/conftest.py reads `RESULTS_DB` (default `/var/tmp/llm-scoring/results.db`) — unused today but relevant if new tests touch it.

report_2.evidence: Shared test-resource collision. `tests/conftest.py:4` defaults `RESULTS_DB` to a single path `/var/tmp/llm-scoring/results.db`. Multiple engineers running `pytest` on the same machine hit the *same* DB, cross-contaminating parallel runs... each worktree exports its own `RESULTS_DB`.
